methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $139M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $3.2B - $8.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Reflection AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $8B

private.name: Together AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $3.3B

data.narrative:
Sygaldry Technologies is a Series A–stage AI & Software company founded by Chad Rigetti that has raised $139M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparison universe of nine private companies and one public peer (Palantir, ticker PLTR), with particular focus on four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables: Reflection AI ($8.0B Series A), Physical Intelligence ($5.6B Series B), Together AI ($3.3B Series A), and Black Forest Labs ($3.2B Series B).

Two triangulation methods were applied. The stage-progression method, using the $139M total raised as a baseline proxy and applying a typical 2.0x Series A step-up multiplier, yields an implied valuation of $278M. The peer-median method across the four comparable companies produces a median valuation of $4.5B, with a range spanning $3.2B to $8.0B. These methods reflect markedly different assumptions about growth trajectory and relative positioning within the frontier AI sector.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $236M (low) to $5.1B (high), with a median estimate of $2.4B. The wide spread reflects material uncertainty regarding whether Sygaldry's development stage, market traction, and competitive positioning align more closely with the stage-progression baseline or the peer-median benchmark. Key uncertainties include revenue trajectory, product-market fit evidence, and whether the peer set adequately reflects sector-specific risk and growth volatility.
